title: Severe Traumatic Brain Injury
chief_complaint: Unrestrained driver in high-speed MVC. Found unconscious at scene. Intubated by EMS. GCS 6 at scene (E1 V1 M4).

physical_exam.Neurologic: GCS 5T (E1 V1 M3). Right pupil 5mm, sluggish. Left pupil 3mm, reactive. Decoritcate posturing on right, decerebrate on left.
physical_exam.Head: Right frontal scalp laceration. Raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis - anterior skull base fracture). Hemotympanum (blood behind tympanic membrane).
physical_exam.ATLS: A: Intubated. B: Equal breath sounds bilaterally. C: Tachycardic, normotensive. D: As above. E: Abrasions on chest and abdomen.

hidden_data.ct_head: Right frontal cerebral contusion with surrounding edema. Multiple punctate hemorrhages at gray-white matter junction (diffuse axonal injury - DAI). Subarachnoid hemorrhage in basal cisterns. 3mm midline shift. Right temporal bone fracture.
hidden_data.icp: ICP monitor placed: Opening ICP 35mmHg (normal <15). CPP = MAP - ICP = 80 - 35 = 45 (goal >60).

correct_diagnosis: Severe Traumatic Brain Injury (GCS 5) - Cerebral Contusions, Diffuse Axonal Injury, SAH, Elevated ICP (Refractory)
key_learning_points: Severe TBI = GCS ≤8. Secondary brain injury from hypoxia, hypotension, hypercapnia, hyperthermia, seizures, and cerebral edema is PREVENTABLE and worsens outcomes significantly

ICP management: Tiered approach: 1) HOB 30°, head midline, sedation/analgesia, EVD for CSF drainage. 2) Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV or Hypertonic saline 3% (target Na 145-155). 3) Neuromuscular paralysis (Cisatracurium), Mild hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 - temporary), MAP augmentation (pressors for CPP >60). 4) Barbiturate coma (Pentobarbital - burst suppression on EEG, requires continuous EEG monitoring). 5) Decompressive craniectomy (controversial - DECRA and RESCUEicp trials)

Avoid: Hypotension (SBP <90 doubles mortality), Hypoxia (SpO2 <90%), Hyperthermia (increases ICP), Hyperglycemia, Hyponatremia (cerebral edema). Maintain euvolemia. Early enteral nutrition (within 7 days). DVT and stress ulcer prophylaxis

Diffuse axonal injury (DAI): Shearing injury at gray-white matter junction from rotational acceleration-deceleration forces. CT often normal or subtle petechial hemorrhages. MRI with DWI/SWI more sensitive. Poor prognosis: Prolonged coma, persistent vegetative state

Prognosis: GCS 5 = 50-60% mortality. Factors: Age (worse >60), GCS motor score, pupil reactivity (best predictor), CT findings (compressed cisterns, midline shift), DAI. ICP control does NOT guarantee good outcome
